output_text:
Summary: Revised the text to remove an internal contradiction (the "first algorithm" claim conflicts with the cited Formaggia–Perotto 2003 work), sharpen the contribution against prior art, and tighten the prose.

**Key issue flagged before the rewrite:** The original asserts GoalAniso is "the first algorithm to combine goal-oriented DWR with anisotropic mesh refinement," then cites Formaggia and Perotto (2003) doing exactly that on the same problem class. As written, this is self-refuting and a reviewer will pounce on it. The rewrite below reframes the contribution as a specific architectural distinction (dual solution drives metric *directions*, not just element sizing) rather than a primacy claim. If you genuinely have a primacy claim, it needs to be narrower and stated precisely — e.g., "first to use the dual solution itself, rather than the primal Hessian, to set anisotropic directions."

---

**Revised text:**

Adaptive finite element methods for convection-dominated PDEs need error estimators that capture both the magnitude and the directional structure of solution features. Layers in these problems are intrinsically anisotropic, so isotropic refinement — even when driven by a sharp goal-oriented estimator — wastes degrees of freedom resolving directions in which the solution barely varies. Existing anisotropic strategies, in turn, typically derive element shapes from the primal Hessian (Frey and Alauzet, 2005) or from element-wise anisotropic residuals (Formaggia and Perotto, 2003), neither of which aligns refinement with the directions that matter for a specified quantity of interest.

We present GoalAniso, an adaptive scheme in which the dual solution to a goal functional plays a dual role: it weights the residual, as in the dual-weighted residual (DWR) framework of Becker and Rannacher (2001), and it sets the principal directions of the anisotropic metric used to shape elements. The local DWR contribution sets the metric's scale; the dual gradient sets its orientation. This is the distinction from Formaggia and Perotto (2003), who weight an anisotropic residual estimator by dual information but derive element directions from primal-side quantities, and from Hessian-based anisotropic adaptation, which is oblivious to the goal functional.

At each adaptive iteration, GoalAniso solves the primal and dual problems on the current mesh, assembles the goal-aligned metric, and refines elements with the largest DWR contribution along the resulting directions.

We evaluate on three convection-dominated benchmarks: a rotating-pulse problem, a 2D boundary-layer problem at Péclet 10^4, and the Hemker test problem. To reach a fixed functional accuracy on Hemker, GoalAniso uses 41% fewer degrees of freedom than goal-oriented isotropic refinement and 28% fewer than Hessian-driven anisotropic refinement; reductions on the rotating-pulse and boundary-layer problems are of comparable magnitude (full tables in Section 5).

These results suggest GoalAniso is a practical default for goal-oriented adaptivity when layer structure dominates the error.

---

**Recommendations not folded into the rewrite (decide and apply):**

1. **Verify the Formaggia–Perotto framing.** I rewrote based on your description of their paper, but if their estimator does set element *directions* from dual information, my contrast collapses and the contribution needs to be restated again (perhaps as: extension to the Hemker class, better metric construction, sharper analysis). Re-read their §3–4 and confirm.
2. **Replace "of comparable magnitude"** with the actual percentages once you have them — vague suite-wide claims are weaker than two more numbers.
3. **Consider adding one sentence on cost.** Reviewers will ask whether the dual solve plus metric construction eats the DOF savings. A line like "the per-iteration overhead vs. isotropic DWR is X%" preempts that.
4. **"Practical default"** is stronger than "practical option" but still soft. If the numbers hold across the suite, say "outperforms both baselines across the suite" in the closing line.
5. **Title/name.** "GoalAniso" reads fine; just confirm it isn't already taken in the FEM literature.

Uncertainty: I have not verified the Formaggia–Perotto (2003), Becker–Rannacher (2001), or Frey–Alauzet (2005) citations against the actual papers, nor confirmed that the methodological contrast I drew (dual-driven directions vs. primal-Hessian or primal-residual directions) accurately distinguishes your method from theirs. Recommendation 1 is the load-bearing check.
